excerpt_en: After school, I had a meeting with GPS runner Shimizu-san. I got to know him during the COVID pandemic, and he invited me to participate in many events. On top of that, during the 2023 Taiwan cycling project, we spent a full month together
body_en:
After school,

I had a meeting with GPS runner Shimizu-san.

I got to know him during the COVID pandemic,

and he invited me to participate in many events.

On top of that,

during the 2023 Taiwan cycling project,

we spent a full month working together.

My role was recording video and photos,

building the crowdfunding website,

and creating masu gifts for the people in Taiwan.

We meet regularly,

and we even met up in America.

And then, for the first time since America, we had an online meeting.

The topic was about his next project.

He has introduced me to so many people in the Kansai area,

and I've decided to do whatever I can for him.

During our online meeting,

we hammered out a lot of details for the next project.

🔻To the Irish Chamber of Commerce in Japan

I received an invitation

and attended a Chamber of Commerce event.

When I arrived at the meeting time,

everyone except me was in a suit!!!

Both the Japanese and Irish people

were all dressed formally.

I was the only one dressed casually,

and even wearing sneakers. Lol

I definitely felt out of place,

but I prioritize personality

over appearance,

so no problem!

Anyway, I just kept networking with everyone, over and over.

The main event was

a talk by important figures from Japan and Ireland,

and after that ended,

everyone was free to mingle.

I had prepared a lot of FOMUS content, but contrary to my expectations, there was no space to display things, so I had slipped just one masu into my pocket beforehand.

This strategy worked out great,

a huge success!!

When I introduced myself,

the question everyone always asks is

"What are you doing here?"

So when I pull out the masu, since it's an unusual line of work,

people find it fascinating.

Just like in Japan, older people

tend to find it more interesting than younger ones.

Since there were hardly anyone younger than me there,

in a way, it really hit the mark.

I also happened to meet someone from the Irish Embassy, and one of their missions is

"to promote Japanese sake in their country."

I see, indeed, Japanese sake is

a traditional Japanese drink,

and spreading it is one of their missions.

This was great information to hear! Lol

Our content is about the "vessel" for sake,

and the reality is that Japanese sake hasn't spread much in Ireland yet.

If somehow, through an embassy project, they could hold a Japanese sake event, and the masu could be used as the vessel -- that would be amazing!!

How could I get the embassy involved in organizing an event?

This is definitely worth pursuing.

Furthermore, the people who came here

are people who have been active in Ireland for a long time,

some living in Ireland for 10 to 25 years.

Also, in Ireland,

there are several communities,

each relatively small,

and I was able to meet someone

who is trying to connect them all together.

Week 11 in Ireland.

I feel like my world has expanded once again.

Indeed, events organized by embassies and chambers of commerce attract significant people, and you have to seize every opportunity like this!

Looking back after the event,

I networked quite a bit, but I think I could have pushed even harder and deeper.

That said, meeting people

who are definitely worth being connected to

was incredibly valuable.

Also, another great thing about this event was that

wine and food were provided during the networking time.

Free of charge, of course.

Having this kind of service

is really appreciated!

I personally work better

with a little alcohol in me,

so I quickly got some white and red wine flowing. Lol

That was a nice assist! Lol

🔻To the Japanese Meetup

Since it was Wednesday,

I headed straight to the weekly

Japanese meetup as well.

I go almost every week,

and since I also organize my own events,

I naturally know more people now,

and that initial outsider feeling is completely gone.

Having someone I know there makes a big difference, and even though I've only been here for 11 weeks,

being able to blend in with people who've lived here for 3 to 8 years is really something I'm grateful for.

I hate to say it myself,

but I think I'm moving faster than anyone in Ireland.

If I were just another person on a working holiday, it would be hard to even get people to remember my name, but having the masu and running my own business gives me an identity that regular working holiday people don't have, and I'm making full use of it.

What's also interesting is that

the people I've connected with

almost all know my Instagram.

That's why they also know that I'm organizing international exchange events in Dublin.

That's why conversations move fast! Lol

Because I went all out,

the rewards have been substantial.

And so,

at this meetup too,

I made the rounds to various groups,

and it was a very meaningful time.

Maybe being in Dublin on Wednesdays

and going to other countries on other days is one approach! Lol

So that's the story of

going all in because there's nothing to lose!

title_en: To a Sushi Restaurant in Ireland
excerpt_en: Went to language school again today. I didn't have any particular business plans in the afternoon, so I attended the free class. *It's for the teacher's practice, so it's free for students. I'm really grateful for that. It actually started on Monday, but
body_en:
Went to language school again today.

I didn't have any particular business plans in the afternoon,

so I attended the free class.

*It's for the teacher's practice, so it's free for students.

I'm really grateful for that.

It actually started on Monday,

but meetings in Japan kept overlapping

so I hadn't been able to attend.

I finally made it! Lol

Being able to spend 2.5 hours from 1:40 to 4:00

on additional English study

is the best for me.

🔻To Matsukawa

And then, I finally went to "Matsukawa."

It's a high-end sushi restaurant.

I first heard about this place in my first week in Ireland,

at a Japanese meetup event,

when someone told me about a restaurant run by a Japanese owner,

and I had been planning to visit at some point.

Then, about a month before this,

I met the owner at a Japanese meetup,

and we've been getting along well,

so I decided this was the time to go.

I did want to eat sushi,

but more importantly, figuring out how to get FOMUS placed there was a much higher priority,

and when proposing the masu, I can't suggest where it could be used without actually having eaten there, so that was another reason.

The owner wasn't there today,

but I did get to meet the head chef who makes the sushi.

I had reserved the 5 PM slot,

and there were two other foreign guests.

With foreigners sitting next to me,

chatting away in Japanese felt a bit awkward, so I focused on the meal.

We were eating at the same pace,

and they left right after finishing their meal.

Now was my chance!! Lol

I was the only customer,

and no one would come until the next time slot!

Meaning I had a chance for conversation,

and after finishing my meal, when the timing felt right for both the chef and me, we had a nice chat.

What I learned was that

there are no direct flights from Japan to Ireland,

so importing fresh fish is difficult.

Currently, except for yellowtail and tuna,

they use fish caught in Ireland.

Wasabi can be grown in Ireland too,

but the taste isn't great,

so they import it from Shizuoka.

Also, since sushi uses raw fish,

freshness is crucial,

and unlike Japan,

fresh fish doesn't arrive consistently in Ireland,

so maintaining quality is really challenging.

Indeed, when I ate at the high-end sushi restaurant Oribe in Malaysia, the portions were bigger

and there was more variety.

*FOMUS masu are placed there too.

That's how much harder the taste challenge becomes.

Here's the menu.

I took photos of every dish.

It looks like this.

There were a few other varieties too.

Honestly, I've been to high-end sushi restaurants several times,

so I think I have a decent palate.

Being able to eat sushi in Ireland is wonderful in itself,

and since it's aimed more at international customers rather than Japanese, I think this is fine.

They're clearly putting in a lot of effort on the taste,

and a novice like me has no place to comment on that,

but I found two areas where FOMUS could contribute!

Those are:

The masu as a vessel for Japanese sake, and

the masu as a vessel for desserts.

This time, I ordered Japanese sake,

and it came in a fairly large Edo Kiriko glass,

which made the amount I ordered look underwhelming.

The glass wasn't even half full.

It's actually the right amount,

but because the glass is so big,

you naturally get this illusion of it being insufficient.

If you just change the glass size,

this wouldn't happen.

If you had a masu here,

people could enjoy even that experience,

and you could pour the same amount to the brim,

which would definitely increase satisfaction.

Furthermore, if you could do a "mokkiri" where sake overflows outside the masu,

people would start taking photos and videos.

Currently, they're just pouring sake into a glass,

so I thought maybe there should be some entertainment element?

And the second thing.

The final dessert.

Honestly, this was just average.

You might say "well, it's a sushi restaurant," but I could tell even before it was served that they weren't putting much effort into the dessert.

The monaka was fine,

but the strawberries and ice cream were ordinary.

If they used a masu for the plating here,

it would look much more stylish.

And on top of that, it would be Instagram-worthy!!

This is where

I felt FOMUS could step in.

After eating sushi that they work so hard to maintain the quality of, if the dessert is just ordinary, the overall impression inevitably fades.

This is something the pastry chef was often told

when I worked at a French restaurant,

that even if you serve the best French cuisine from appetizer to main course, whether the customer leaves happy ultimately

comes down to the dessert.

The last taste you have

determines the overall satisfaction of the entire meal.

I heard this when I was working in a French restaurant at a hotel, and I thought it was absolutely right.

No matter how delicious the meat course is,

the dessert and coffee are what

close out the meal,

and this part is incredibly important!

That's why, as FOMUS,

I think these two areas are where I could improve Matsukawa's flavor and satisfaction.

Honestly, there's nothing FOMUS can do about the sushi toppings themselves. *Maybe at best, a vessel for the pickled ginger.

Since people are coming all the way to Ireland to eat sushi,

and some do order Japanese sake,

I'd really love for them to enjoy it with a masu, a traditional Japanese craft.

And with this same approach,

I could also pitch FOMUS masu to restaurants

that serve Irish whiskey.

I still haven't done enough research,

but there seems to be a lot I can do,

so I'll give it a try.

So yeah, that was my

report on eating sushi.

Also, the head chef was born in '95, same age as me.

After we chatted, we exchanged contact info.

Once again, I'm glad I've been doing activities centered around the masu, and because I had experience working at a French restaurant in a hotel, I can think about how to elevate a restaurant's quality even further.

Indeed, all activities connect to each other.

Now, from here, I'm going to shift into higher gear

and seriously start developing restaurants that will use FOMUS!

excerpt_en: After the morning class ended, I quickly had lunch and headed to the afternoon free class. I have FOMUS work to do, but improving my English is also an important part of all this, and my time at language school is running out, so I'll attend
body_en:
After the morning class ended,

I quickly had lunch

and headed to the afternoon free class.

I have FOMUS work to do,

but improving my English is also an important part of all this,

and my time at language school is running out,

so I'll attend classes as much as possible.

The free class is for

intermediate level and above,

so most people can speak reasonably well.

Plus, it's multinational,

so unlike the Japanese classroom style,

when the teacher asks a question,

everyone speaks up with their own opinions.

This is totally different from the Japanese approach.

It's not a question of which is better,

but it's interesting how

culture and environment change this attitude.

Of course, I also actively share my opinions in class.

*The complete opposite of what I did in school back in Japan.

Including all of that, it's a great learning experience.

🔻Card Game Event

This Wednesday, there was a Chamber of Commerce event, so this week only, I held the card game event on Friday.

Five people had reserved.

In the end, only one person actually showed up.

The other one was Yaoki, who goes to the same language school.

He's been participating since the very first card game event I organized.

When there are a lot of people,

he also helps support the event.

But today, there were only 3 of us total.

So we did play the card game,

but since I wanted to value the time for conversation,

we chatted a bit longer than usual.

With 3 people, the conversation flows very naturally.

My role is to make sure everyone thinks this time was fun, and that they want to come again next time.

That's why, regardless of the number of people,

I give my all to make the event exciting.

The person who came was from Ireland,

and told me their sister married a Japanese person and lives in Yokohama.

Being able to hear stories like these

is something that would never happen

if I didn't organize events myself.

You really have to treasure every encounter.

🔻Oktoberfest at Night

After SILVA wrapped up,

I headed to a one-night-only

Oktoberfest held in Dublin.

The organizer is someone who plans events in Dublin,

and since he's German,

it was an Oktoberfest theme.

Basically, it was a regular party.

I paid the 20 euro (about 3,500 yen) entry fee

and went in.

Drinks were pay-as-you-go.

The flow was to mingle freely.

The only Japanese there were me and Yaoki (who joined on the spot).

There were people from

Hungary, Ireland, India, and more.

If Japanese people stick together they'll end up speaking Japanese,

and I'm not here to be comfortable,

so I just went up to people and started talking.

I wanted to stand out, so I came wearing a masu around my neck, and lots of people asked "What's that?" which helped me spread the word about FOMUS.

About an hour and a half in,

pizza was served.

However, there were about 80 people, and

since it was standing only,

the moment pizza was placed on the table, people swarmed in,

and within a minute, most of it was gone.

This kind of thing is a "first come, first served" situation,

and I don't have that typical Japanese "holding back,"

so I jumped right into the pizza battle! Lol

#I paid for this after all

I really wanted to take photos,

but there was no time for that.

There were 3 rounds of pizza,

and I won all three battles.

I successfully secured 3 slices of pizza

while also enjoying the socializing.

At this party,

rather than selling FOMUS,

the value was in being part of a space

where people paid 20 euros to gather.

Also, the fastest way to improve English

is to put yourself in an environment where you have to speak it, so attending events with no safety net is also fun.

Being in an environment where I know zero people,

where nothing starts

unless I speak up first,

is stimulating and fun.

Whether you get high or low satisfaction

for the same money you pay is up to you.

Same goes for the pizza battle. Lol

Yaoki and I went in together but split up once inside to mingle separately,

and before I knew it, he had already left.

I stayed until the very end, of course.

I wanted to spend as much time

speaking English as possible.

That said, the bingo and quiz events

before and after the main event,

I personally could have done without. Lol

#conversations stop during those

From a business perspective,

tickets were 20 euros per person with about 80 attendees.

That means about 1,600 euros in sales.

Since the venue was rented out exclusively,

even if the rental was 200 euros,

that's still 1,400 euros in profit,

and since drinks were paid separately,

the venue cost might have been zero.

In that case, the revenue goes straight to the organizer.

A pretty significant amount of money moved in just a few evening hours.

1,600 euros for just 3.5 hours is substantial.

Even if the scale is smaller,

it would be amazing if FOMUS

could organize similar events.

If we could serve Japanese sake, beer, wine, and whiskey, with all drink sales going to the venue,

and in exchange get the venue for free

or at 10%,

that would be ideal.

If the entry fee is 20 euros and 20 people attend,

FOMUS handling the draw would get 400 euros in revenue.

And for the venue,

if everyone can drink at 7 euros per drink,

each person would have 1 to 3 drinks,

about 15 euros per person.

With 20 people, that's 300 euros in sales.

The profit would be around 250 euros.

If sales come in without having to do any marketing,

that's great for the venue too,

and this negotiation might be worth trying.

If this works,

with a weekly event limited to 20 people

and 400 euros in revenue, that's 1,600 euros per month.

If it's just me,

I can easily live on that revenue.

Plus, people I become friends with at these events

could lead to masu-related collaborations,

further expanding the network.

It's actually quite viable.

And since I'm running it as FOMUS,

the masu serves as our differentiator.

In my first month in Ireland,

I participated in various meetups to gauge the demand for events and social gatherings.

In the second month, I used that data to think things through, organized walking and card game events as an organizer, and got decent results.

In the third month, I want to try events with higher price points, and it seems like the right time to start organizing drinking events.

Maybe I'll start small?

So, thanks to participating in this party,

I could see a lot from a business perspective.

I'll make sure to put these insights to good use!!

excerpt_en: This past month, weekdays were at language school, afternoons were meeting people or doing online meetings, attending free afternoon classes when there was time, and on weekends, walking events in the morning, card game events in the afternoon, or participating in other meetups
body_en:
This past month, weekdays were at language school, afternoons were meeting people or doing online meetings,

attending free afternoon classes when there was time,

and on weekends, walking events in the morning,

card game events in the afternoon,

or participating in other meetups,

basically, I was constantly socializing with people.

In other words,

every single day, I was interacting with someone.

Honestly, if it's for spreading FOMUS,

socializing isn't a burden for me,

and I actually enjoy it.

But when I spend too much time socializing,

I can't find time for desk work

or thinking about business on my own.

This isn't great for me,

because sometimes I need to sit down and strategize.

And that can only be done when I'm alone.

So today, even though it's Saturday,

I decided to skip my own events

and dedicate the day to focused work.

Right now, I'm working between Japan and Ireland, and things have gotten busier than expected.

On top of that, in Ireland,

I'm not making great money

from masu, art, or photography,

and since rent in Ireland is about twice that of Japan, I'm dipping into my personal savings.

Until now, weekday mornings were taken up by language school,

so I've been using afternoons and weekends

to push things forward,

but it's about time to build

a revenue stream in Ireland.

So I went to a nearby library

and spent the whole day

thinking about how to make that happen.

Right now, I'm also simultaneously working on

a new Japanese project,

so there's a mountain of things to think about.

Having alone time for the first time in a while, without seeing anyone (well, I do talk to my share house mates), was refreshing in a way.

And of course, I got a decent amount of work done too.

Since this is the paid version, I'll share what

I was thinking about today -- mainly three things.

1. Art Gallery Project in Japan

2. Drinking Event Strategy for Ireland

3. FOMUS Subscription Service (domestic and international)

Today, October 12, 2024, I was racking my brain,

and I'm sharing my thoughts on potential breakthroughs.

I can't put this kind of stuff out publicly. Lol

*Please keep the following to yourself.

1. Art Gallery Project in Japan

We're currently planning an art gallery using shipping containers on Sagi Island in Hiroshima Prefecture.

Activities will officially start

in November this year,

and we need to have it open

by March.

That means in six months,

we need to restore a beat-up container,

prepare the gallery exhibits,

and on top of that,

work collaboratively with the island residents on the project,

use the gallery as a catalyst

to attract more visitors to the island,

all while delivering outstanding quality.

If this gallery succeeds,

we could be commissioned for

renovation projects of vacant houses and containers in other locations.

FOMUS has already organized

an art gallery in Saitama Prefecture,

bringing in 100 visitors over 2 days.

If this Sagi Island gallery

takes off,

it opens up the possibility of developing new locations.

That's why

producing this art gallery

absolutely cannot fail.

After calculating the costs,

container repairs and art installations

will require about 1 million yen.

*Without doing anything, it would just be a loss.

So developing a business model to recoup this is FOMUS's (my) role.

First, we want people to recognize it as a gallery

and take the ferry to come visit,

so creating a space that makes people

want to post on social media is a must.

In other words, a space that makes you want to take photos.

From my travels around Japan and abroad,

there's one type of space that always makes me take photos.

That's ceiling art.

This is a Japanese-style space that,

thanks to the stylish ceiling art,

makes you instinctively want to take a photo.

This time, using FOMUS's strength in AI,

we plan to create a massive ceiling art piece that covers the entire container ceiling with art.

The great thing about this is that

anyone from children to elderly people

can participate.

And you don't need any artistic skill.

Because it's AI-generated,

drawing ability isn't needed.

Rather,

how you craft your words is more important.

After calculating, we'll need about 160 pieces, and ideally, we'd like about 100 people to help create this massive art piece.

By selling the right to display works

in this ceiling art,

we're hoping to reduce the deficit.

There's more, but I'll save that for later.

2. Drinking Event Strategy for Ireland

Next up, business in Ireland.

Personally, a business that lets me

connect with new people,

build networks,

and also generate revenue

is ideal for me right now.

In that case,

photography work and event planning

would be my main pillars.

Also, as I wrote in yesterday's article,

at the Oktoberfest event in Dublin I attended, 80 people participated at 20 euros each, generating 1,600 euros in just a few hours.

That's truly impressive.

Even if the scale is smaller,

I wanted to create a higher-quality event

myself.

So,

I changed the name of my walking event group

on the meetup app.

It was originally "Make Friends & Language Exchange: walk & talk"

but I changed it to

"Make Friends & Language Exchange:

Ireland🇮🇪"

I officially changed it to an Ireland event group name.

Until now, it was a walking group

doing walking events,

but now,

as an international exchange group for Ireland,

I can still do walking events

plus other types of events too.

I was able to make this move because

the walking group exceeded 560 members

and the card game group exceeded 400 members,

so it can sustain itself without being tied to one type of content.

So,

I immediately planned a drinking social event.

Named "Make Friends & Language Exchange: Drink&talk🍻"

It just changed from walking to drinking.

I don't know how much demand there is, so I'll just try it next week.

Again, I'll charge 5 euros per person, handle the marketing,

and create an environment that connects people.

Also, unlike other drinking groups,

it's limited to 10 people, and with FOMUS masu,

the Baby Guinness shots I tried a little while ago

were just the right size,

and I thought combining them with the masu would be fun!

By the way,

Baby Guinness is a popular shot cocktail served in a shot glass that looks like a pint of Guinness beer, hence the name. It's not actually beer but a cocktail made with liqueur. It features a layered design that looks like a mini Guinness, making it a party and event favorite.

Since it's a sweet cocktail,

if I serve dark chocolate and nuts inside the masu as snacks (paid from the entry fee),

it should differentiate us from other events.

I'll start with 10 people max,

gradually increase,

and aim for about 20 regulars each time.

At first it'll probably be 3

or 5 people,

but there are various ways to work with this,

so I'll give it a try.

So,

I've already scheduled two sessions for next week!

Let's see how it goes!!

3. FOMUS Subscription Service (domestic and international)

And the third thing

is building a FOMUS subscription service.

I've been thinking about this for a while.

Over these 3 years, I've been active in many ways,

traveled to many places,

and met many people.

And I've learned that there are so many fascinating people doing interesting things in the world.

And the people I find interesting

also find me interesting.

And when interesting people I know connect with each other,

even more interesting things happen -- I've confirmed this.

They're already meeting regularly without me,

and I've been able to create a good environment.

So I want to accelerate this even more,

deepen the connections,

and since I'm in Ireland

and can't easily organize events in Japan,

I want to clearly share the services and content that FOMUS and I currently offer with FOMUS subscription community members,

and if members can also exchange or sell their skills, I feel like we could do even more interesting things.

Eventually, I'd like to transition to tokens, but jumping straight to tokens is difficult, so first I'll start a monthly community that only paid members can join.

I can prepare various perks,

and if I can list subscription members' products and services

as member perks

at a slight discount,

that would be even more interesting.

And beyond that,

since I'm overseas,

I'm thinking of building a monthly subscription service in Ireland too.

It's difficult right now to provide enough value to justify joining,

but I could translate my newsletter into English,

do monthly online meetings,

monthly members-only gatherings at Japanese restaurants, and there are various other things I could do.

Also, this is still uncertain,

but I mentioned meeting someone who works at the embassy when I attended the Chamber of Commerce event this week. When I told them I wanted to do something to spread Japanese culture and crafts,

that person gave me the email address of the embassy's PR person.

They probably told the PR person something like

"There's someone here from Japan spreading culture."

I'm currently trying to schedule a meeting, and I don't know if I'll get a reply,

but connecting with someone at the embassy before even 3 months in is great.

They'd probably ignore an email from someone like me

if I sent it from Japan,

but actually being here is a significant point.

Anyway,

since I have some pretty interesting material in both Japan and Ireland, I could use that as newsletter content that people would find engaging,

so launching subscriptions simultaneously in Japan and abroad might actually work.

Also, the biggest advantage is that

a subscription model stabilizes revenue,

which makes planning more consistent.

This is what FOMUS needs right now,

and if members stay subscribed long enough,

when we eventually launch token utilization,

we can increase the initial token distribution based on their subscription duration, so there are various possibilities.

Before I knew it, while thinking about all this,

the day flew by.

Tomorrow is the walking event.

30 people have reserved,

but like last week

when 17 people reserved and none showed up,

the actual turnout is unpredictable.

I hope at least 10 come!!

Well, let's see what happens!!

excerpt_en: 10/13 Last day of week 11 in Ireland! It's Sunday, so time for the regular morning walking event! Today, 12 people from 10 countries participated. The group wasn't super large, so we walked around Trinity College in the city center
body_en:
10/13

Last day of week 11 in Ireland!

It's Sunday,

so time for the regular morning walking event!

Today, 12 people from 10 countries participated.

The group wasn't super large,

so we walked around Trinity College in the city center.

I didn't do any scouting beforehand,

so I relied on Google Maps,

but since it was Sunday,

some roads were closed,

and we ended up taking a weird route. Lol

Still, we walked for about an hour,

everyone having a good time.

Having people gather like this

is not something to take for granted,

as I learned last week,

and for each person who shows up,

I need to make sure they feel

"I'm glad I came!"

"I want to participate again somewhere!"

That requires thoughtful effort.

One of those efforts

is the post-walk social.

Usually, we use Starbucks,

but the Sunday Japanese meetup event

starts at 12:30,

and if I hold my event's social at the same spot,

it would forcibly end at 12:30,

and to avoid mixing with the Japanese meetup,

I held the post-walk social at the venue

where I usually host card game events,

about a 5-minute walk from Starbucks.

This was the first time trying this,

and thanks to changing the social venue,

we could keep the walking event social going

until about 1:30 PM.

In other words, we were able to have more shared time, and since everyone was aware that they had participated in the walking event together,

it was easier to become friends.

I reaffirmed that

I should cherish this event.

🔻Things That Would Never Happen in Japan

Since Ireland has people

from many different countries living here,

unlike in Japan,

it's normal to ask "Where are you from?"

after saying hello.

If you run a regular event in Japan,

90% or even 100% would be Japanese,

but Ireland is different.

Through international exchange with them,

I gain more practical English skills,

my communication abilities improve,

I learn about their countries,

and they learn about FOMUS and about me,

so it's all positives!

I'm fulfilling my role as organizer

while genuinely enjoying it.

That seems to come through

to the participants,

and the event reviews are really great.

I'm truly grateful!

🔻Dropping by the Japanese Meetup

After my own event ended,

I headed to the Japanese meetup.

This is a Japanese meetup organized by someone from overseas, not me.

That said,

since Japanese people and people who love Japan gather,

it's interesting in its own way.

For me, the Sunday Japanese meetup

serves international exchange, sure,

but my main goal is talking to Japanese people.

For information gathering,

meeting new people,

and occasionally,

for the chance to meet

interesting students or working holiday folks.

If you don't invest money and time in encounters,

nothing starts,

so I go all in.

Then, at the end of the event,

there was someone I talked to

early on when I first came to Ireland,

who has been living in Dublin for about 5 years,

and I went up to them right away.

The conversation kept going,

and they took me out for drinks.

There, we had a pretty deep conversation

about each other's lives.

It's nice to have moments like this sometimes!

In the end,

I was socializing non-stop from 10 AM to 10 PM.

#Didn't even have time for lunch

#Voice is holding up fine though

If even one more person

can learn about me and FOMUS,

I'll keep going as long as it takes,

and again,

I got useful information this time too,

so I'll follow up on it.

And so,

at my own events, I do international exchange,

at others' events, I build Japanese connections,

and at both, I spread awareness of FOMUS --

this combination

consumes a massive amount of energy and time,

but that's how strongly I can imprint myself and FOMUS on everyone,

and I feel that taking action yourself

is the most important thing of all.

Being able to have these experiences

is thanks to the people around me supporting me,

and I'll continue to spread FOMUS

further across Europe!

I'm going all out in week 12 too!!

excerpt_en: Week 12 of life in Ireland has begun!! It's been almost 3 full months since I came to Ireland. Time really does fly! As for my upcoming schedule, today is 2024/10/14,
body_en:
Week 12 of life in Ireland has begun!!

It's been almost 3 full months

since I came to Ireland.

Time really does fly!

As for my upcoming schedule,

today is 2024/10/14,

language school ends on the 18th ☑️

Current house contract ends on the 20th 🏠

Starting the 21st, one week in Estonia 🇪🇪

From the 28th, Halloween in Ireland 🎃

From November 3rd, Malta 🇲🇹

From November 9th, some country? (haven't decided yet)

Mid-November, traveling around Galway and Cork,

and from December, I plan to be fully based in Dublin for activities.

In other words, after the 20th,

I don't have a place to live! Lol

That said, I'll be in other countries for some of that time,

so I'll just stay in hotels, no problem.

From December, I'll need to find a place to live.

So what I've been working on behind the scenes

is a plan for a Japanese share house.

Renting one house for six months

and sharing it would be relatively cheap,

and if people just need a place to sleep,

we could also offer accommodations.

Also, from my perspective,

if I can use the house for parties,

I could generate revenue there

and reduce the rent cost.

I wondered if anyone would be on board with this plan,

but it turns out

there are already people around me

who think it's a great idea.

I've found 3 people,

all Japanese, all in favor of the share house,

and all willing to actively participate in events!

I'm really grateful!

That said, even if the members are set,

if we can't find a house by December,

this whole plan falls apart.

Plus, it's mid-October now,

so there's about a month and a half, and from the homeowner's perspective,

they'd prefer someone who can move in right away,

so we'll probably find a house sometime in November.

So today,

I held a meeting to share this information

and do a face-to-face meeting

with the potential share house members.

We did talk about the house,

but even more than that, deepening their bonds

was my personal mission for today.

I didn't need to dominate the conversation,

so it turned into a fun social gathering.

We shared things like why each of us came to Ireland,

and discussed strategies for finding a house.

I think it was a productive meeting and introduction.

🔻Video Shoot

I had another plan in the evening.

That was dinner and a video shoot

with Haru-san, who's been in Dublin for 2 years now.

She wanted to film

Irish stew,

and I got to film a FOMUS masu x Irish stew video too.

With that plan set,

after the face-to-face meeting, I invited the Dublin House members along, and everyone headed to a pub.

#Love how everyone's so quick on their feet

We ordered

delicious Irish stew,

fish and chips, and more.

From business talk to favorite types,

it was a genuinely fun drinking session for the first time in a while.

I think it's important to value these kinds of breaks, and meeting these people in Dublin

is a lifelong treasure,

reminding me once again how important connections are.

For the first time in a while, I didn't have to run anything,

and I genuinely enjoyed myself.

We called it a night around 10 PM,

and I walked home for about 40 minutes.

It was supposed to just be the Dublin House meeting,

but the two girls who came along

to the video shoot and pub afterwards -- huge thanks!

It was a time that made me feel

like I need to push even harder!!

I'm going all out in week 12 too!!

excerpt_en: After language school, I headed to the hotel I often use for work. This is also where I hold the SILVA card game events. I did nothing but desk work there. Then at 6 PM, card game event time! Two people showed up today. Lately, reservations have been
body_en:
After language school,

I headed to the hotel I often use for work.

This is also where I hold the SILVA card game events.

I did nothing but desk work there.

Then at 6 PM,

card game event time!

Two people showed up today.

Lately, reservations haven't been coming in much and it's been tough!

That said, having people participate at all

is something I'm truly grateful for,

but for an exchange event

with so few people,

as the organizer,

I honestly want to make it more lively.

Once again, I feel that attracting people is tough.

Still, including Yaoki,

a Japanese guy who always comes to the card game events,

the four of us

played today.

With SILVA,

the rules and explanations are fine.

What I need is

better marketing and repeat visitors.

People aren't coming back,

so the reality is that people are just enjoying it once.

I need to come up with strategies

to drive repeat attendance.

🔻To the Wednesday Meetup

After the event, I went to the Japanese meetup.

The turnout was low today.

I always order beer regardless.

The usual crowd was thin today too,

so I decided to go check out some new faces.

My mission is to get as many people as possible to know about FOMUS and MaSU,

so I actively approach people.

My communication skills really do improve from this! Lol

🔻Getting More Consultations Lately

And what's interesting is that

lately, more people have been coming to me for advice.

I've only been in Ireland for 12 weeks,

so there are plenty of people who know more than me,

but I get consulted a lot.

What kind of consultations?

Mainly two types:

"I have something I want to do, but how should I go about it?"

"What should university students be doing?"

These two come up especially often.

90% of these come from university students,

and 10% from working holiday folks.

Yeah, these aren't Ireland-related questions,

they're life advice and questions about how to start events.

I've already established myself as

"the guy who organizes international exchange events in Ireland,"

and for some reason, quite a lot of people know about me.

Some even told me, "I've always seen you around but could never find the right moment to talk to you."

I thought I was pretty approachable,

but since I'm always talking to someone, maybe the timing is hard to find?

So today,

I ended up doing advice sessions again.

A female college student who wants to organize events

but is in the process of securing a venue

came to me for advice.

Nothing's been decided yet,

but she wants to do events,

so I shared how I run my events.

This stuff really comes down to experience,

and I often answer these kinds of consultations.

But being approached like this

makes me happy,

and from their perspective,

maybe there's a slight barrier to approaching me.

When I first came here too,

I felt a little intimidated

approaching people who had been in Dublin

for 3 to 8 years.

In my case, I participate in other events

and run my own business,

so I could break through easily.

For a typical university student,

approaching me might have a slightly

higher barrier.

That said, I'm nobody special,

so I'm always welcoming.

And so,

I'm finding myself in more situations

where people come to me for advice.

But I'm still a challenger myself,

so I'll keep gathering information.

excerpt_en: After language school, I head to a cafe and do FOMUS work non-stop. FOMUS work never drops to zero -- there's always something to do, and if I don't properly visualize importance and urgency, things get out of hand.
body_en:
After language school,

I head to a cafe and do FOMUS work non-stop.

FOMUS work never drops to zero,

there's always something to do,

and if I don't properly visualize importance and urgency,

things get out of hand.

Then, at 6 PM,

as a trial event,

I held my very first pub event!

🔻Why did I plan this event?

As I mentioned in a previous article,

Ireland has been extremely cold lately.

I expected fewer people would come to morning walking events,

so I needed to create new plans.

In the end, Ireland has a deeply rooted

pub and beer culture,

so I figured indoor exchange events would be strong.

Also, in my first month,

I participated in tons of meetup events

and went to plenty of pub venues,

but they were all incredibly loud! Lol

There's music, but even more than that, the places are packed, making it hard just to talk to someone.

And since the communication is in English,

the listening environment is terrible. Lol

With that experience,

I needed to find a quieter pub, and it turned out that a pub I went to for a friend's birthday party was relatively quiet and Guinness was 5 euros cheaper than other places, so that's how I chose it.

There are other reasons too,

but what's especially important is that

at a pub, I can actually use the FOMUS masu!

My goal is to spread FOMUS,

and organizing events is just a "means" to that end.

I always keep that in mind.

So I planned the pub event, and the big day arrived!!

And the turnout was... 3 people to start!

Me, a Japanese girl, and a Chinese guy.

These people had already

met at last week's walking event.

In a way, there was a sense of comfort. Lol

Why was the turnout so low?

Partly because my marketing power is still weak,

and partly because I haven't created the situation where people think

"I want to drink with MaSU!"

or "I want to drink with the people MaSU is connected to."

Pub events are everywhere,

and for most people, drinking with close friends is more fun.

This is an international exchange event

designed for making friends even between strangers, and it was hard to get the numbers.

Also, this time,

there were so many no-shows who had reserved,

so I implemented a system for pre-paying the participation fee online.

However,

this actually raised the barrier to entry.

So while pre-paying online works for events among Japanese people,

for small-scale international events,

it's apparently difficult.

I tried this format for both today's Thursday event

and this Saturday's pub event with pre-payment,

but people just weren't hitting the join button,

so this approach

doesn't work in the current situation.

Saturday only had 1 reservation,

and it was a Japanese person,

so there was no point holding the event,

and I cancelled it.

This was completely my mistake,

and now that I have this data,

from next time, I'll go back to

the usual style of paying on the day only, just like the walking events.

That said, the 3 of us socialized,

and since we happened to all be in our late 20s,

we had a blast talking about

games and manga.

Also, since I charge a participation fee for this event, I used it to buy snacks and served them in the masu at the pub!

This way,

I can naturally promote the masu,

and give people the realization

that "oh, you can use it like this too!"

So with more people,

the snacks would be even more impressive. Lol

By the way, the candy in this photo

is a famous Irish candy,

it takes a while to finish,

and didn't pair well with beer. Lol

The chocolate balls I also bought

went great with Guinness though.

I might organize another pub event in late October as a Halloween event.

Anyway, after the 3 of us chatted for about 2 hours, my Italian friend Stefano and his friend joined us!

That made 5!!

5 people at the first event

is pretty great!!

And 5 people is just the right number for conversation!!

So if I cap it at 10 and expect

half to show up, 5 is a great conversation size.

Even if only 2 come,

that means deeper conversations,

so I'm in a position with various options.

About 3 hours after the event started,

the first 2 people left,

and then people kept bringing more people, expanding the circle.

The great thing about pubs is that

you can't tell who knows who, but a quick intro and a cheers later, you're friends.

So by that point, my event vibe was long gone, and it was just a regular drinking session! Lol

Before I knew it, 2 Italians, a Turkish guy, and I were toasting together,

and somehow I got treated to whiskey.

Then 2 Spanish people, a guy and a girl, joined,

and suddenly 6 of us were toasting!! Lol

This is also part of the pub experience,

and even if I host an event and nobody comes,

I learned I can just blend into another group!

There were a few moments I could have left,

but by choosing to stay

and keep communicating with them,

I noticed things I wouldn't have otherwise,

and many people smoke.

I don't smoke, so I just drink, but

when you toast with the smokers, the venue shifts from inside to outside, and at this time of year in Ireland, it's basically winter, so it's freezing! Lol

Just from hosting one pub event,

I learned so much,

and based on this,

I can work on

the marketing problem,

snack sourcing,

how to keep things lively,

how many people to aim for,

whether the venue fits the scale,

whether FOMUS can be promoted naturally,

and so on.

A lot of useful data came out of this event.

For me, as long as I get data,

the event is a success,

and while marketing was tough,

the value exceeded that.

I'll use this

for future pub events,

and ultimately,

I want to toast with FOMUS's secret weapon, the "BOMAS."

title_en: Language School Graduation and Visit to the Embassy
excerpt_en: Today marks Friday of week 12 in Ireland. And so, I officially graduated from the language school in Ireland today! Three months went by incredibly fast. My language study in the Philippines was also 3 months,
body_en:
Today marks Friday of week 12 in Ireland.

And so,

I officially graduated from the language school in Ireland today!

Three months went by incredibly fast.

My language study in the Philippines was also 3 months, and in a way, the time in Ireland felt like it passed faster since the class hours were shorter.

As for whether my English improved

from studying abroad,

honestly, it's debatable,

but going to school allowed me to meet people I never would have otherwise,

and meeting people was why

I went to school in the first place.

So in my mind, going to school helped

get FOMUS known by many people,

and I'm glad I went.

After school ended,

I had lunch with the usual crew

and headed straight to a pub for a toast! 🍻

Diego, a Chilean who started and graduated at the same time as me,

and I became really close.

We were in the same class at first,

then I moved up quickly and we separated,

but we both kept leveling up,

and ended up in the same class again.

Then I moved up again,

and he followed a few weeks later,

so we started and ended in the same class,

which is a great memory.

We took a commemorative photo together at the end!

The great thing about language study abroad

is making friends

with people from different countries,

and they become lifelong friends.

We all have different jobs,

but if we all grow,

someday we might work together,

and even if not,

we can visit each other's countries as friends.

Over the past 3 years, I've traveled all over Japan,

so I can show people around various places

and introduce them to interesting people I know everywhere.

This was impossible 4 years ago,

so I'm glad I hustled

during the COVID era.

He's heading to Portugal soon,

but I'd love to go to Portugal next year

and meet up again!

🔻Seriously Spreading the FOMUS Brand

With school done,

my weekday mornings

that had been completely blocked off

are now free.

And coincidentally,

after graduating and toasting with everyone,

I was the only one who left early

to head to the Japanese Embassy in Ireland.

The reason was simple.

"I want to collaborate with the Japanese Embassy, involving FOMUS."

At the Japan-Ireland Chamber of Commerce event the other day, I met someone who works at the embassy,

they found me interesting,

introduced me to the embassy's PR person,

I sent an email right away,

and today, we met in the form of a meeting.

You don't get to visit an embassy very often,

so this was a valuable experience.

At reception, I had to fill in various details like my name.

The security was intense.

*Well, of course it is.

Then I met the person in charge,

and at a nearby cafe, we talked about FOMUS,

the embassy's side of things,

and information about Ireland

that you can't find online.

This was incredibly valuable to me,

and they were also interested in FOMUS's activities.

I felt that if I didn't push now, things would never start,

so I went ahead and asked,

"Would there be any way we could collaborate?"

They told me there's an embassy event coming up,

and asked if I'd like to participate.

Being able to join an already-planned event

is an incredible offer,

so I immediately checked the dates.

It's in November, next month,

and by a miracle, it starts just 2 days

after I get back from my Mediterranean trip!

The schedule just barely doesn't overlap! Lol

I immediately said I'd participate,

and if the person in charge can explain it well to their superiors,

I should be able to join the event.

However, sales are not allowed there.

*Cultural exchange is the main focus.

That kind of short-term money

doesn't matter to me at all.

The merit of participating in a Japan-Ireland cultural exchange event at the embassy is 100 times greater,

and I absolutely want in!!

This kind of chance doesn't come often,

and I have nothing to lose,

so all I can do is dive in!!

Honestly, at first I thought it might just be

a meet-and-greet where we talk about FOMUS and Ireland

and that's it,

but this was an unexpected turn of events!!

Once again, a business built on traditional Japanese crafts and culture, combined with completely different genres that other companies and individuals can't replicate, and above all, content that visibly demonstrates overwhelming action -- all of this left a great impression.

This "content that visibly demonstrates overwhelming action"

is what I've been building steadily over 3 years --

Masu Photo,

involving people from all over the world

to hold a masu and take photos,

and compiling them into physical photo books.

The credibility is incredible.

3 months ago,

I came to Ireland alone,

knowing nobody,

showed up at all kinds of events,

got as many people as possible to know I exist,

got them interested,

got invited to more events,

connected with embassy staff there,

got introduced to a Ministry of Foreign Affairs PR person for Japanese culture,

and in a short meeting,

presented FOMUS and myself,

and got them interested.

As for the presentation, I've done FOMUS presentations and explained my reasons for choosing Ireland countless times in Japan, so I understand exactly what word choices and content resonate -- it all comes down to experience.

Of course, the November event could still be

rejected by the embassy's higher-ups,

and I wouldn't be able to participate,

but if it doesn't work out,

I'll just propose the next opportunity myself,

so there's no real downside.

Besides, the fact that a nobody

went aggressively to the Japanese Embassy

and presented his own brand

is already a step forward.

It took 3 months,

but I managed to connect with the embassy.

Building a network from scratch to get here,

I've felt firsthand how important connections are,

and I hope this conveys

the importance of "connections" to everyone reading this.

What FOMUS is aiming for

is to spread these connections not just in Japan but worldwide,

and the tool for that is the "FOMUS masu."

So today,

I graduated from the language school I attended for 3 months,

and went straight to the embassy to present FOMUS!

Tomorrow is Saturday, so I'm hosting

both a walking event and a card game event!

There is absolutely no time to rest!! Lol

I'm shifting into even higher gear and going all out!!

title_en: Behind the Scenes of the Walking Event
excerpt_en: It's Saturday, so walking event time! Today Ireland had clear skies for the first time in a while, and 15 people participated -- just the right number! Since the weather was great, I figured a park walk would be nice, so
body_en:
It's Saturday,

so walking event time!

Today Ireland had clear skies for the first time in a while,

and 15 people participated -- just the right number!

Since the weather was great,

I figured a park stroll would feel amazing,

so we swung by a park I often visit

and walked together!

The college student who had come to me for advice on running events also participated this time,

and she seemed to get a feel for

"So this is how you do it!"

I'm grateful that

people from various countries participate,

which improves my English,

lets people learn about FOMUS,

and helps me make friends worldwide.

And the participants connect with

each other the same way,

and compared to other events,

we provide more time for in-depth conversations,

so the connections become deeper.

So, not to pat myself on the back,

but this is a very high-satisfaction event,

and participants are incredibly grateful.

The reason is that

everyone seems to attend other meetup events too, but those are usually at pubs at night,

where it's too loud to have proper conversations.

Plus, since it's a drinking venue,

people who don't drink but want to socialize find it hard to join.

My walking event

has no alcohol, it's in the morning, it's not loud,

and I make sure everyone gets one-on-one conversation time,

so for people who genuinely want to make friends,

it's the ultimate content.

Plus, the organizer is Japanese,

and everyone wants to visit Japan at least a little,

so there's this mysterious sense of security

that "if it's a Japanese person's event, it'll be fine."

Also, as I wrote before,

they always form two lines,

and I hand out paper slips with "alphabet and number" at the start, so based on those, everyone lines up neatly at the beginning.

*Details like this matter when organizing internationally.

*It's about leadership.

Of course, getting to this point

took lots of trial and error,

so none of it was wasted.

Recently, I had always been starting from A for the slips, but that meant I had to make new ABC papers for every event, so starting from the previous event, I now continue from where the last event left off.

That means sometimes it starts from A,

sometimes from G,

and this time it started from M.

The lineup chart only takes 3 minutes

to update,

so I no longer have to write out alphabets late at night.

Efficiency matters!!

*This time it started from M.

So after 2 months of running events,

as long as enough people show up,

the walking event is an extremely high-satisfaction format.

And it's not just me saying this --

everyone leaves reviews and ratings,

so I can confirm it.

We consistently maintain a 4.6 rating (out of 5),

and even a 3 is considered good,

so these are excellent results.

After the walk,

we socialize at a cafe,

and those I didn't get to talk to during the walk,

I connect with there.

One thing I noticed is that

people surprisingly don't move around to talk to different people, and personally, I wish they would connect with more people.

That said, it was a very meaningful event.

🔻Card Game Event Struggling?

After the walking event,

it was straight to the card game event.

I chatted with the walking participants

until 10 minutes before the event started,

then rushed to the next venue.

This time, 6 people had reserved,

but only 1 person actually showed up, from Spain.

The card game really takes off with about 10 people, so I really want to work harder on marketing.

That said, one-on-one, we played SILVA

and enjoyed the conversation.

She was from Spain,

and since I've been to Spain too,

we had plenty to talk about.

I don't spend a long time talking about someone's country,

but just having visited it

closes the distance,

so I want to keep traveling to various countries.

🔻Day Before Moving Out

After the events, I went home

and immediately did laundry.

The washing machine in my share house

is broken and unusable,

so I'd have to go to the laundromat at a nearby supermarket,

but it was always such a hassle

that I ended up hand-washing everything at home.

But I'm heading to Estonia the day after tomorrow,

and I'm not taking my suitcase,

so I wanted my clothes to be clean.

I've been staying at this place for 28 days,

and on day 28, I finally went to the laundromat for the first time! Lol

*Before this, I was in a homestay, so they did the laundry.

So, my first laundromat experience! Lol

I didn't know how to use it at first and struggled,

but I managed to get it going.

Washing and drying combined was 1,600 yen,

which is a decent price,

but for a whole month's worth of laundry, it's super cheap!

I save where I can.

Then I mostly finished packing too.

I need to return the key at 11 AM tomorrow,

so I made sure to prepare properly.

This share house was supposed to be a single bed

but turned out to be bunk beds

in a 4-person room, and I was like, this isn't what I signed up for!!

But in the end, it's just a place to sleep,

and while there was some stress,

I managed to live there for a month.

*It never quite became home sweet home though.

Unlike a hostel,

staying in a share house for more than a week

was a first for me,

and it was a good experience in its own way.

And I realized once again that

I can handle this lifestyle while I'm young,

but I couldn't do this at 40.

*You can't fully recharge.

*Zero private space.

So, my month-long stay at Cabra in Dublin 7

ends with tonight's sleep!

I bought various groceries,

and I should be able to use everything up cleanly

with tonight's dinner and tomorrow's breakfast before moving out.

*My scheduling game is perfect on this front.

I'll probably write about it in the next article too,

but from here until December, I won't have a fixed base

and will be living the nomad life.

Unlike when I traveled around Japan,

this will be nomad life crossing borders,

which is pretty exciting!

Next week I'll be in Estonia,

so at this timing,

I also started recruiting Masu Photo models in Estonia.

In the past, when I recruited early,

people would make other plans

and cancel,

so I'm recruiting at this last-minute timing.

If I can shoot 10 or more people, I'll have enough for a photo book,

so I really want to get at least 10!

Keep an eye on this!!
